config.title: Stasi Museum Berlin - FAQ
config.subtitle: Tickets, opening hours, and the difference between the museum and the prison.

items.q: What is the Stasi Museum in Berlin?
items.a: The Stasi Museum is a research and memorial centre inside House 1 of the former headquarters of the Ministry for State Security (the Stasi) on Ruschestraße in Berlin-Lichtenberg. This was the actual nerve centre of East Germany's secret police. You walk through the preserved office suite of Stasi chief Erich Mielke, see the surveillance equipment, disguises and hidden cameras the ministry used, and read how the system worked. It is run by an association of civil-rights campaigners, not a commercial attraction.

items.q: Where is the Stasi Museum and how do I get there?
items.a: The address is Ruschestraße 103, 10365 Berlin, in the Lichtenberg district east of the centre. The easiest way there is the U5 to Magdalenenstraße, which is about a 300 metre walk from the museum and roughly 15 minutes from Alexanderplatz. The old ministry complex is huge, so follow the signs to House 1 once you arrive.

items.q: How much are tickets and what are the opening hours in 2026?
items.a: Admission is 12 euros for adults and 9 euros reduced, with cheaper rates for teenagers and school groups. The museum is open Monday to Friday from 10:00 to 18:00, and weekends and public holidays from 11:00 to 18:00. It is self-guided, but an English-language guided tour runs at 15:00 on weekdays for an extra 5 euros. Always check the museum website before you travel, since times can change.

items.q: Is the Stasi Museum the same as the Stasi prison?
items.a: No, and this trips up a lot of visitors. The Stasi Museum on Ruschestraße is the former ministry headquarters and offices. The Stasi prison is a different site: the Gedenkstätte Berlin-Hohenschönhausen at Genslerstraße 66, the secret police's main remand prison, where people were interrogated and held. They are several kilometres apart in the same district. If you want the interrogation cells and the human story, that is Hohenschönhausen, not the museum.

items.q: Can you visit the Stasi prison, and do you need a tour?
items.a: Yes, the Hohenschönhausen memorial is open daily from 10:00 to 18:00, but you can only see the cell blocks and interrogation rooms on a guided tour. Tours run several times a day, including English ones, and cost around 9 euros. Many are led by former prisoners, which is what makes the visit so powerful. Book online in advance, because you cannot simply walk through the prison on your own and popular slots fill up.

items.q: Is the Stasi Museum worth visiting?
items.a: If you are interested in the Cold War, divided Germany, or how a surveillance state actually functioned day to day, yes. The museum is calm, detailed and central-ish, and Mielke's untouched office is a genuine time capsule. If you have limited time and want the more emotional experience, choose the Hohenschönhausen prison instead. On a short trip, do one of the two rather than trying to fit in both.

items.q: How big was the Stasi, really?
items.a: By 1989, its last full year, the Stasi employed 91,015 full-time officers and ran roughly 189,000 unofficial informers (inoffizielle Mitarbeiter) who reported on neighbours, colleagues and family. It held files on about a third of the East German population. When the regime collapsed, staff shredded documents by hand; about 16,000 sacks of torn pages survive and are still being pieced back together. That scale is exactly what both the museum and the prison help you understand.
